book_metadata.title: Hedge Fund Market Wizards: How Winning Traders Win
book_metadata.author: Jack D. Schwager
book_metadata.focus: Hedge fund managers across macro, systematic, and equity strategies

traders.name: Colm O'Shea
traders.fund: COMAC Capital (formerly at Soros Fund Management)

performance.notes: Managed money for Soros; specific returns not disclosed in text

key_rules: Formulate hypothesis, then test it against market action - if market disagrees, hypothesis is wrong
Participate in bubbles from the LONG side, never short a bubble until it breaks
Implement trades via asymmetric structures (options, CDS, TED spreads) where max loss is defined
Set stops at the price where your thesis is disproven, not at your pain threshold
If stop implies uncomfortable loss, reduce position size rather than tightening stop
Trade implementation can be more important than the trade idea itself
Long bonds instead of short stocks when expecting equity bear market (avoids bear market rally whipsaws)

traders.name: Ray Dalio

performance.max_drawdown: Worst year was negative single digits
performance.notes: Manages $100B+, largest hedge fund in the world

key_rules: Build 100+ uncorrelated return streams across all asset classes
All Weather portfolio: 4 quadrants (growth increasing/decreasing x inflation increasing/decreasing)
Depression gauge: monitor interest rates, credit growth, stock market, credit spreads simultaneously
Timeless and universal approach - if it only works in one period or one market, reject it
12-18 month average holding period
Always stress-test against 1930s depression scenario
Deleveraging cycles behave differently from recessions - same policy responses have different effects
Mistakes are essential learning opportunities - document every one

traders.name: Larry Benedict

performance.notes: 19 of 20 years profitable, only losing year was -0.6%

key_rules: 100-200 trades per day, extremely active market maker style
3-day rule: after 3 consecutive up/down days, look for reversal
Monthly drawdown limit: at 2.0-2.5% loss, liquidate ALL positions and trade tiny until recovered
Intermarket hedging: use correlated instruments to offset directional risk
Never trade to reach a profit target - only trade when opportunity presents itself
Scale positions based on conviction and market conditions

traders.name: Scott Ramsey
key_rules: Develop fundamental bias (bullish/bearish), then use technicals for precise entry
Buy strongest instrument in sector, sell weakest (intermarket divergence)
Risk only 10 basis points (0.1%) per individual trade
Use RSI divergence to detect trend exhaustion
200-day MA as primary trend filter
Fibonacci retracements for entry levels on pullbacks
Watch how correlated markets respond - divergence signals opportunity
Commodity vs equity divergence as leading indicator

traders.name: Jaffray Woodriff

performance.notes: Systematic futures trading, hundreds of models

key_rules: Neither pure trend following nor pure mean reversion - uses 1000+ models from secondary variables
Derive signals from transformations of OHLC data (not raw prices)
Aggressive cross-validation to prevent overfitting: test on out-of-sample data ruthlessly
Volatility-adjusted position sizing across all models
Cut exposure by 75% when volatility quadruples (as in 2008)
Continuous research and model evolution - static systems degrade
Diversify across many weakly correlated models rather than concentrating on few strong ones

traders.name: Edward Thorp

performance.notes: Never had a losing year in 20 years of fund management; pioneer of quantitative finance

key_rules: Kelly criterion for position sizing: bet half-Kelly to reduce volatility while capturing most of the growth
Convertible arbitrage: buy underpriced convertible bonds, short the stock to hedge
MUD strategy (evolved to statistical arbitrage): buy underperforming stocks, short outperforming stocks
Sector-neutral then factor-neutral portfolio construction to isolate alpha
60-day lookback for correlation matrix in stat arb
Trend following with fundamental filters as supplementary strategy
Arbitrage trades: do NOT reduce on drawdowns (theoretical max loss is defined)
Directional trades: DO reduce exposure on drawdowns (open-ended risk)
Systems must adapt or degrade - went through 3 major iterations of stat arb

traders.name: Jamie Mai

performance.notes: Concentrated portfolio, 15-20 positions, 50-80% cash, featured in The Big Short

key_rules: Exploit 5 failures of Black-Scholes pricing model assumptions
Assumption 1: Returns are NOT normally distributed (fat tails exist)
Assumption 2: Forward price is NOT necessarily future mean (trends persist)
Assumption 3: Volatility does NOT scale by square root of time (regime changes)
Assumption 4: Volatility is NOT trend-blind (trending markets have different vol profiles)
Assumption 5: Correlations are NOT stable (they spike in crises)
Buy cheap sigma: options that are cheap because models misjudge the true probability
Binary outcome scenarios: when either big up or big down is likely, options are systematically underpriced
Hold 50-80% cash, concentrate on 15-20 high-conviction asymmetric positions
Wait for high-conviction trades - doing nothing is a valid strategy

traders.name: Michael Platt
traders.style: Multi-Strategy (Discretionary Fixed Income + Systematic Trend Following)
key_rules: 3%/3% trader risk rule: lose 3% from starting allocation = halve exposure; another 3% = remove all allocation
Traders are structured like options: limited downside, unlimited upside
Annually rebasing stop levels (not trailing)
LIBOR-OIS spread as early warning indicator for liquidity crises
Systematic trend following: exit overextended trends BEFORE reversal signals (response curves)
Neutral to long volatility always - never short out-of-the-money options
Monitor correlation breakdown (especially in spread positions - lower correlation = higher risk)
Ask each trader daily: where would your biggest $10M loss come from?
Daily re-evaluation: would you enter this trade today at this price? If no, exit
Market maker mindset: hire people who understand edge, not analysts with ego
80/20 rule: 80% of profits come from 20% of trades - protect ability to catch those trades

traders.name: Steve Clark

performance.notes: Profitable every year since 2001 inception. Worst year was +0.7% (2011)

key_rules: Do more of what works and less of what doesn't - analyze P&L by trade type
Position size > entry price: if position keeps you awake, it's too big
Trade within your emotional capacity
Screen for unusual volume as a signal that something is happening
When everything lines up, swing big - even if wrong, won't be wrong by much
If position behaves in a way you don't understand, cut it immediately
On losing streaks: liquidate everything, take a holiday, restart small
Cut exposure by 75% when volatility quadruples
Really good traders change their mind in an instant
Staring at screens all day leads to overtrading - find productive distraction
Beware euphoria: if you're hysterical to buy more, that's the signal to sell

traders.name: Martin Taylor

performance.annual_return: 27%+ annualized compounded net across full career (1995-2011)
performance.notes: Only losing year was 2008 (-17%), vs EM index -54%. Voluntarily cut AUM by 75%.

key_rules: Three requirements for a long: favorable macro, secular trend, good company
Always high-beta stocks balanced by cash or shorts - never boring low-beta
Net exposure range: 20% net long (floor) to 110% net long (max)
Never go fully net short - if you're out completely when it reverses, you'll never get back in
RSI only useful as OVERSOLD indicator (extreme oversold = take a look); useless as overbought
Charts as supplemental: if fundamentally bullish but stock is overbought, start small (1% vs 10%)
Breakouts confirm fundamental thesis - go to full position when chart confirms
If stock massively underperforms market, check if management lied about fundamentals
Forecast earnings 3-4 years out; buy when cheap on forward earnings, not current
Capital flight analysis: match reserves data with reported trade flows to detect fraud
Gut feel is crucial: if price action doesn't match fundamentals, recheck everything

traders.name: Tom Claugus
traders.style: Contrarian Value / Mean Reversion Exposure Management

performance.personal_account: 33% gross annualized over 7 years prior to fund
performance.notes: Net short during most of 1990s bull market yet still profitable. 5 losing years in 26.

key_rules: Mean reversion exposure model: use 95% confidence band on log-price regression since 1932
At lower band: 130% long / 20% short (110% net long)
At midpoint: 100% long / 50% short (50% net long, reflecting secular uptrend)
At upper band: 20% long / 90% short (70% net short)
Apply same band analysis by sector to identify cheap/expensive sectors
Evel Knievel screen: >5x book value AND losing money = short candidates
Look for free optionality: companies priced on current business with unpriced future revenue >1 year out
Screen for extreme earnings changes (>50% up or >30% down) as anomaly detector
If down >7% in a month and don't know why, reduce exposure
For every 1% loss, reduce exposure by 2% (scaling risk down proportionally)
Shorts are easier to find than longs: broken companies easier to spot than good ones

traders.name: Joe Vidich

performance.notes: Through two massive bear markets. HFR Equity Hedge index only 4% annualized in same period.

key_rules: Top-down: big picture economy -> sector themes -> individual stock selection
Listen to 300+ conference calls per quarter for information edge
Market sentiment = price action relative to events (NOT public opinion polls)
Stock down after good earnings report = bearish; stock up after bad report = bullish
Harvest losses: periodically liquidate losing positions to force re-evaluation vs alternatives
Scale into positions and scale out - never try to be 100% right
If stock is down 15%, re-evaluate but don't auto-sell (mental stops, not hard stops)
Start selling 20% of a losing position first - partial loss is easier than full liquidation
Sell on the way DOWN from target, not on the way up (might miss further upside)
Bull market pattern: open low, close high. Bear market: open high, close low. End of trend: pattern flips.
Sideways stock with sudden sharp volume + upmove = wake-up call, investigate

traders.name: Kevin Daly

performance.notes: SP500 was -9% cumulative in same period. Russell 2000 +68%. Remarkable for near long-only.

key_rules: Value screens: EV/EBITDA, Price/FCF, P/E, EV/EBIT (cap rate), Price/Tangible Book for banks
Circle of competence: only invest in easily understandable businesses
Buy a dollar of assets for 50 cents - margin of safety is primary defense
Sell when stock reaches fair valuation - don't hold for tax reasons or hope for more
Go to 60-90% cash when macro environment is uncertain
Monitor esoteric economic data (rail car loadings, truck traffic, hotel RevPAR) for real economy signal
Avoid value traps: skip melting ice cube businesses (newspapers, yellow pages, video rental)
Look for hidden niches inside larger companies that market ignores
Small asset size is an advantage - can trade illiquid small caps others can't
Patience: stayed ~90% cash for 2+ years during 2000-2002 bear market

traders.name: Jimmy Balodimas

performance.notes: Never had a losing year in 15 years. Short ~75% of the time in a market that's up 53% of days. Innate talent, not replicable.

key_rules: ALWAYS take some money off the table when market moves in your favor - even small amounts
Trade around positions: cover 20% of shorts on every down open, re-short higher on rallies
Contrarian: sell into euphoria, buy into panic
Broken stock pattern: stock breaks on high volume, market makes new highs but stock can't reclaim 50-day MA = short
Volume surge on parabolic move = supply entering, likely near top
Flash crash response: don't panic, verify data, then buy into indiscriminate selling
Natural short bias: the marketing machine to be long is unnatural, unsustainable energy
If position feels painful, start reducing - trust physical/emotional signals
Never need to make money in the same stock where you lost it
Adaptation: keep positions smaller until seeing clear transition signal

traders.name: Joel Greenblatt
traders.style: Value Investing / Special Situations -> Systematic Value

performance.notes: Returned all investor capital in 1994 because AUM was impeding returns. Shifted to systematic value.

key_rules: Magic Formula: rank stocks by combined earnings yield (EBIT/EV) + return on tangible capital
Earnings yield = EBIT / Enterprise Value (normalizes for debt and tax rate differences)
Return on capital = EBIT / (Net Working Capital + Net Fixed Assets)
Top decile beats bottom decile by ~15%/year; perfect ordering across all 10 deciles
Value weighted indexes outperform cap-weighted indexes by ~7%/year over 20 years
Equal weighted indexes beat cap-weighted by ~2%/year (cap weighting systematically overweights expensive stocks)
Special situations: spinoffs, restructurings, rights offerings trade at discounts because institutions dump them
Binary outcomes + options = outsized returns (Wells Fargo LEAPS: 1:5 risk/reward on 50/50+ odds)
97% of top-quartile managers spent 3+ years in bottom half - past performance has ZERO predictive value
Value investing works BECAUSE it doesn't work all the time - periodic underperformance keeps edge alive
If you can't value a company, skip it - there are no called strikes on Wall Street
6% minimum hurdle rate even at zero interest rates (opportunity cost of future better deals)

strategies.name: Macro Hypothesis Testing Framework
entry_rules: Form a macro hypothesis based on economic fundamentals
Look for asymmetric implementation: options, CDS, spreads where max loss is defined
Wait for market action to confirm hypothesis before sizing up
If market disagrees with hypothesis, abandon thesis (market is right)
exit_rules: Set stop at the price that disproves the thesis (not at pain threshold)
If stop implies too large a loss, reduce size instead of tightening stop
risk_management: Maximum loss defined by option premium or spread structure
Position size calibrated to thesis conviction and stop distance
strategies.notes: Framework rather than precise rules. Powerful concept of thesis-first, asymmetric implementation.

strategies.name: All Weather Portfolio (4-Quadrant Model)
entry_rules: Classify current regime into one of 4 quadrants: growth increasing/decreasing x inflation increasing/decreasing
Allocate to assets that perform well in each quadrant
Growth increasing + inflation increasing: commodities, inflation-linked bonds, emerging market credit
Growth increasing + inflation decreasing: stocks, corporate bonds
Growth decreasing + inflation increasing: inflation-linked bonds, commodities
Growth decreasing + inflation decreasing: nominal bonds, stocks at lower weight
exit_rules: Rebalance as regime shifts between quadrants
risk_management: Risk parity: equalize risk contribution across quadrants, not dollar amounts
Diversification is the Holy Grail - 100+ uncorrelated return streams
strategies.notes: Conceptual framework described at high level. Bridgewater's actual implementation is proprietary and far more complex.

strategies.name: Mean Reversion Correlation Trading
entry_rules: After 3 consecutive up days, look for short entries on strength
After 3 consecutive down days, look for long entries on weakness
Use correlated instruments to hedge: if long S&P, hedge with short sector ETFs showing relative weakness
exit_rules: Take profits quickly on mean reversion bounce
If losing 2.0-2.5% in a month, liquidate ALL positions immediately
risk_management: Monthly drawdown circuit breaker: 2.0-2.5% = flatten everything
Trade very small after hitting monthly limit until recovered
100-200 trades/day provides natural diversification
strategies.notes: High-frequency discretionary mean reversion. The 3-day rule is codifiable. The monthly circuit breaker is the real innovation.

strategies.name: Intermarket Divergence Strategy
entry_rules: Develop fundamental directional bias for a sector
Buy the STRONGEST instrument in the sector, sell the WEAKEST
Confirm with RSI divergence at extremes
Enter on Fibonacci retracement pullbacks toward the 200-day MA
Wait for technical confirmation: price must be respecting the 200-day MA as support/resistance
exit_rules: Exit when fundamental bias changes or intermarket relationship normalizes
Stop loss at point where thesis is invalidated
risk_management: Risk only 10 basis points (0.1%) per trade
Spread between long and short in same sector reduces directional risk
strategies.notes: Most codifiable macro strategy in the book. The 10bp risk per trade is extremely conservative.

strategies.name: Secondary Variable Pattern Recognition System
entry_rules: Generate 1000+ models from secondary variables derived from OHLC data
Secondary variables = transformations, ratios, moving averages of price data (not raw prices)
Each model generates small alpha; combine many for robust signal
Aggressive cross-validation on out-of-sample data before deployment
exit_rules: Model-driven exits
Cut exposure by 75% when market volatility quadruples
risk_management: Volatility-adjusted position sizing across all models
Extreme volatility = massive exposure reduction
Diversification across hundreds of weakly correlated models
strategies.notes: Conceptual framework only. Specific models are proprietary. Key insight: neither trend nor mean reversion - a third way.

strategies.name: Kelly Criterion Position Sizing
entry_rules: Calculate Kelly fraction: f* = (bp - q) / b where b=odds, p=win prob, q=loss prob
Use HALF Kelly to reduce volatility while retaining most of long-term growth rate
Half Kelly reduces growth rate by only 25% but cuts volatility by 50%
risk_management: Never exceed full Kelly - severely penalizes long-term growth
Overestimating edge is worse than underestimating it
For arbitrage (bounded risk): no need to reduce on drawdown
For directional (unbounded risk): reduce exposure on drawdown
strategies.notes: Mathematically precise and well-documented. Challenge is accurately estimating win probability in trading (vs gambling).

strategies.name: Statistical Arbitrage (Market-Neutral)
entry_rules: Buy recent underperformers, short recent outperformers (MUD: Most Up, Most Down)
Sector-neutral: equal dollar exposure long and short within each sector
Factor-neutral: hedge out common factor exposures (size, value, momentum, etc.)
60-day lookback for correlation matrix
exit_rules: Mean reversion target: exit when relative performance normalizes
Time-based exit if reversion doesn't occur within expected window
risk_management: Market-neutral portfolio construction
Factor hedging eliminates systematic risk
Must continuously adapt - went through 3 major iterations as market evolved
strategies.notes: Well-described conceptual framework. Specific implementation details are proprietary. Key warning: must continuously adapt.

strategies.name: Cheap Sigma / Mispriced Distribution Options Strategy
entry_rules: Identify situations where Black-Scholes assumptions fail (see 5 failures above)
Buy options when true probability of large move exceeds model-implied probability
Focus on binary outcome scenarios: either big up or big down is likely
Hold 50-80% cash, only 15-20 concentrated positions
exit_rules: Options expire worthless (max loss = premium paid)
Take profits when thesis plays out or option value increases substantially
risk_management: Max loss defined at entry (option premium)
Large cash position provides safety buffer
Only trade when perceived edge is very large
strategies.notes: Excellent framework. The 5 Black-Scholes failures are a systematic way to find mispriced options. Highly relevant for today's markets.

strategies.name: Magic Formula (Systematic Value)
entry_rules: Rank all stocks by Earnings Yield: EBIT / Enterprise Value
Rank all stocks by Return on Tangible Capital: EBIT / (Net Working Capital + Net Fixed Assets)
Combine rankings: add both rank numbers, lower combined rank = better
Buy top 30 stocks from top 1000 by market cap
Rebalance annually, stepping through monthly
exit_rules: Hold for 1 year then replace with new top-ranked stocks
For tax purposes, sell losers before 1 year, hold winners past 1 year
risk_management: Diversification: minimum 20-30 names in portfolio
6% minimum hurdle rate (opportunity cost floor)
Accept 2-3 year periods of underperformance as necessary for long-term edge

indicators.name: Return on Tangible Capital
indicators.formula: EBIT / (Net Working Capital + Net Fixed Assets)

strategies.notes: Most fully codifiable strategy in the book. Backtested 23 years, doubled S&P 500 returns. Perfect decile ordering. Can be implemented mechanically.

strategies.name: Mean Reversion Exposure Bands
entry_rules: Calculate 95% confidence band on log-price regression (data from 1932)
At lower band: go 110% net long (130% long, 20% short)
At midpoint: go 50% net long (100% long, 50% short)
At upper band: go 70% net short (20% long, 90% short)
Apply same analysis to S&P 500, Nasdaq, and Russell 2000; take composite
exit_rules: Continuous: exposure adjusts as price moves within bands
risk_management: If down >7% in a month and can't explain why: reduce exposure
For every 1% loss, reduce exposure by 2%
Use sector-level bands for additional refinement
strategies.notes: Highly codifiable exposure management system. Made 18% annualized in the 1990s while being net short. The 95% band width was refined from 90% after 1999 experience.

strategies.name: Evel Knievel Short Screen
entry_rules: Screen for: Price > 5x Book Value AND Company is losing money
These companies are trying to 'jump the Grand Canyon' - most won't make it
Short the stocks that meet both criteria
Normally yields ~60 names; 180 names signaled extreme bubble in late 1999
exit_rules: Cover when stock declines to reasonable valuation
If stock continues rising despite fundamentals, scale down for every 1% loss (reduce by 2%)
risk_management: Diversify across many short positions
Accept that 1-in-100 times these stocks keep going up (as in late 1999)
strategies.notes: Simple, elegant short screen. Historically very profitable except during extreme bubbles (1999). The count of qualifying names is itself a bubble indicator.

strategies.name: Free Optionality Long Screen
entry_rules: Find companies priced at fair value on CURRENT business only
Identify future revenue sources >1 year out that market assigns zero value to
Examples: unexplored acreage for oil companies, new technology for manufacturers, emerging market expansion
Best entry after a disappointment (sector selloff on unrelated bad news)
exit_rules: When market begins pricing in the future revenue source
When fundamental thesis changes
risk_management: Downside limited to fair value of current business (you're paying fair price for what exists)
Upside enhanced by free optionality of future revenue

indicators.name: Price/Cash Flow
indicators.use: determine if current business is fairly priced

strategies.notes: Claugus calls this his single most important stock selection concept. The key insight is that markets rarely price revenues >1 year out.

strategies.name: Conference Call Sentiment Analysis
entry_rules: Listen to 300+ conference calls per quarter
If stock DOWN after positive conference call = smart money selling, short
If stock UP after negative conference call = smart money buying, go long
If analysts are confused and can't understand the business = opportunity (e.g., Citigroup CDOs in 2008)
If company mentions deferring capex, short their suppliers
exit_rules: Scale out rather than all-or-nothing exits
Harvest losses: periodically liquidate losers to force fresh evaluation
risk_management: Mental stops (re-evaluation points), not hard stops
Different stocks have different risk profiles - Coke gets 5% room, volatile stock gets 50%
strategies.notes: Highly relevant in today's markets with wide conference call access. The 'confusion = opportunity' insight on Citigroup pre-crisis is brilliant.

strategies.name: Deep Value Screen Toolkit
entry_rules: Screen 10,000 stocks using: EV/EBITDA, Price/FCF, P/E, EV/EBIT (cap rate)
For banks: Price/Tangible Book, P/E, Tangible Common Equity/Total Assets
Narrow to ~200 candidates, then qualitative assessment
Circle of competence: only invest in businesses you understand
Look for margin discrepancies between similar companies (competitor analysis)
Track insider buying/selling via Form4Oracle
exit_rules: Sell when stock reaches fair valuation - do not hold beyond
If macro turns uncertain, move to 60-90% cash
risk_management: Buy a dollar for 50 cents (margin of safety)
Good balance sheets and solid free cash flow as quality filters
Avoid value traps: skip melting ice cube businesses
Monitor esoteric economic indicators (rail traffic, truck traffic, hotel RevPAR)

indicators.name: EV/EBIT (Cap Rate)
indicators.use: what you'd pay as a rational buyer

strategies.notes: Extremely well-defined value screening process. Achieved 872% gross vs -9% S&P 500 over 12 years. Small asset size is key advantage.

strategies.name: Spinoff / Special Situations Value
entry_rules: Focus on spinoffs: institutions dump shares because new entity is too small or different sector
Look for insider ownership in the spinoff - if insiders chose the 'bad' company, it's probably not that bad
Calculate leverage effect: low share price relative to total enterprise value = high operating leverage
Read the 400-page documents others won't - complexity is your edge
Other special situations: restructurings, rights offerings, recapitalizations, two-tiered tender offers
exit_rules: Sell when stock reaches intrinsic value
Don't fall in love with any position even if playing with house money
risk_management: Concentrated portfolio (6-8 positions) in original fund
Operating leverage works both ways - monitor fundamentals closely
Use options for binary outcome scenarios to get 5:1 risk/reward on 50/50+ situations
strategies.notes: Greenblatt averaged 50% annually for 10 years using this approach. Limited capacity is the constraint.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Asymmetric Risk/Reward Is the Common Thread
cross_cutting_themes.description: Nearly every trader in the book structures trades for limited downside and open-ended upside. O'Shea uses options and CDS. Mai buys cheap options. Platt structures trader allocations like options (3% max loss, unlimited upside). Greenblatt uses LEAPS for binary situations. This is the single most universal principle across all 15 traders.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Flexibility and Willingness to Be Wrong
cross_cutting_themes.description: Every successful trader described can reverse positions instantly when proven wrong. O'Shea reversed from bearish to bullish in April 2009. Clark emphasizes changing your mind in an instant. Taylor maintained some long exposure even when bearish to avoid getting whipsawed. Rigidity is the common cause of trader failure.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Risk Management Is the Real Edge
cross_cutting_themes.description: None of these traders describe an edge in prediction. Instead, they all describe an edge in risk management. Platt's 3%/3% rule. Benedict's 2.5% monthly circuit breaker. Claugus's 7% monthly loss investigation threshold. Ramsey's 10bp per trade risk. Clark's 'if you're thinking about it at night, it's too big.' The consistent message: surviving to trade tomorrow is more important than today's profit.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Value Investing Works Because It Doesn't Always Work
cross_cutting_themes.description: Greenblatt, Daly, Claugus, and Taylor all demonstrate that value approaches outperform over the long term precisely because they underperform for stretches long enough to drive out impatient capital. The institutionalization of short-term performance monitoring has actually increased the value edge by shortening most managers' time horizons.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Market Response to News > The News Itself
cross_cutting_themes.description: Platt, Vidich, Taylor, and Balodimas all emphasize that how the market responds to news is more important than the news. A stock down on good earnings is bearish. A market that refuses to go down on bad news is bullish. This sentiment reading through price action is a core skill shared across discretionary traders.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Position Sizing Matters More Than Entry
cross_cutting_themes.description: Clark, Vidich, Thorp, and Balodimas all stress that size determines whether you can hold a good trade. Too large = fear-driven exits on noise. The Kelly criterion provides mathematical framework (use half-Kelly). Multiple traders independently arrived at the same conclusion: manage size to stay within emotional capacity.

Thorp went through 3 iterations of stat arb. Platt describes systematic trading as 'a research war.' Balodimas adapted from aggressive to patient contrarian as hedge fund participation increased. Static systems and approaches degrade. Continuous evolution is required.

cross_cutting_themes.theme: Patience and the Willingness to Do Nothing
cross_cutting_themes.description: Mai holds 50-80% cash. Daly was 90% cash for 2+ years during 2000-2002. Greenblatt says there are no called strikes. The willingness to sit in cash when opportunities are poor is as important as identifying good opportunities.

key_concepts.description: Mathematically optimal position sizing. Full Kelly maximizes long-term growth rate but with extreme volatility. Half-Kelly sacrifices only 25% of growth rate while cutting volatility by 50%. Recommended for any strategy where win probability can be estimated.

Simultaneous monitoring of interest rates, credit growth, stock market, and credit spreads to detect transition from recession to depression/deleveraging. Different policy responses are needed for each.

Options are systematically mispriced when: (1) returns have fat tails, (2) forward price != future mean, (3) vol doesn't scale by sqrt(time), (4) vol is trend-dependent, (5) correlations are unstable. Identifying these failures reveals cheap options.
Widening spread between LIBOR and OIS (Overnight Indexed Swap) rate signals banking sector stress and liquidity drying up. Platt used this as early warning before 2008 crisis.

Sum of all monthly returns / absolute sum of all monthly losses. Unlike Sharpe ratio, does not penalize upside volatility. GPR > 1.0 is very good. GPR > 1.5 is excellent. GPR is equivalent to Profit Factor - 1 when applied to monthly returns.

Buy stocks priced at fair value for current business, where future revenue sources (>1 year out) are assigned zero value by the market. You pay fair price but get upside of anticipated future revenue for free.

The institutionalization of short-term performance monitoring has shortened most managers' time horizons. This creates opportunity for those willing to take a 2-3 year view. Stocks with near-term uncertainty are systematically underpriced.

Reduce position on favorable moves, reinstate on corrections. Generates extra profits from chop, makes it easier to hold winning trades through corrections. Drawback: gives up some profit if market moves smoothly in your favor.

Structure trader risk like a long option: strict max loss from starting allocation (3%), but unlimited upside as profits accumulate. Annual rebasing. Creates asymmetric portfolio returns at the fund level.

Cap-weighted indexes systematically overweight expensive stocks and underweight cheap ones. Value-weighted indexes (allocating more to cheaper stocks) beat S&P 500 by ~7%/year over 20 years with same beta and volatility.
